1:
On Numbers 22:1 — “And the children of Israel set forward, and pitched in the plains of Moab on this side Jordan by Jericho” — the witnesses mark Israel encamped on the border of the promised land.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the place. Charles Ellicott: the Arboth Moab, the plains stretching by the Jordan, once Moab’s, taken by Sihon and then by Israel. Keil and Delitzsch: Israel encamped on the very border of Canaan, parted only by the Jordan. The Puritan Henry: their wanderings ended, they camp by Jericho till they pass over. The Reformed Calvin: Satan, foiled in open war, now assails the Church by curses and divination.

The verse sets the scene. Israel, their wanderings done, encamps in the plains of Moab by Jordan. The new danger of Balaam approaches.

So the witnesses see Israel at last brought to the edge of the promised land, encamped in the plains of Moab over against Jericho. The witnesses note the place: “the plains of Moab… by Jericho” — Ellicott and Wesley explain that these plains, though still called Moab’s, had been taken from Moab by Sihon the Amorite and from him by Israel; Keil marks that Israel now stood on the very border of Canaan, parted from it only by the Jordan. They mark the close of the long wilderness journey, which Henry observes: the forty years’ wanderings were ended, and the people camped here by Jericho until they should pass over to the conquest. They observe the new and subtler danger that the chapter opens, which Calvin draws out memorably: that there is no stone Satan will not turn for the destruction of the Church; having assailed Israel in vain by force of arms (Sihon and Og), he now attacks them by another weapon — the curses and divinations of Balaam; the enemy, beaten in open battle, resorts to sorcery and the cunning of false prophecy. They note the providence in the placing: on the threshold of their inheritance, God’s people meet a fresh assault, that his power to bless and keep them might be the more displayed. They observe that the danger of the curse was met by the God who turns curses into blessings (Deuteronomy 23:5). They note that the story of Balaam unfolds here. The verse thus sets the scene on the border — Balak’s fear following in the next verses.
2:
On Numbers 22:2 — “And Balak the son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to the Amorites” — the witnesses mark Balak’s observation of Israel’s victories.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: before the boundary could be passed, much had to be prepared; Balak saw Israel’s deeds. The Reformed Gill: “the Amorites” meaning Sihon and Og both, whose overthrow Balak had seen or heard.

The verse names Balak’s alarm. He saw what Israel had done to the Amorites. The fall of Sihon and Og strikes fear into Moab.

So the witnesses see Balak, king of Moab, take alarm at the report of Israel’s recent conquests. The witnesses note what he saw: “all that Israel had done to the Amorites” — Gill observes that the word “Amorites”, particularly named, takes in both Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan, whose kingdoms Israel had lately overthrown (Numbers 21:24, Numbers 21:35); Balak had seen or heard of these mighty victories. They mark the force of the report on him: the two great Amorite kings, who had themselves taken much of Moab’s land, were destroyed by Israel; and if Israel could so overthrow those who had conquered Moab, what could Moab hope against them? They observe Keil’s note that Israel stood at the border, and that before it could be passed there were preparations on every side — among them this stirring of Moab’s king. They note the working of fear in Balak: it was the sight of Israel’s strength, and the memory of the fallen kings, that set him seeking some way to resist a people he dared not meet in battle. They observe that the conquests God gave Israel served also to terrify their enemies, the report of his works going before his people. They note that Balak’s fear, though it sprang from real events, would drive him to a vain and wicked remedy. They observe that the dread of the nations was itself a sign of God’s presence with Israel. The verse thus names Balak’s alarm — his dread spelled out in the next verse.
3:
On Numbers 22:3 — “And Moab was sore afraid of the people, because they were many: and Moab was distressed because of the children of Israel” — the witnesses mark Moab’s groundless dread.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Moab sore afraid and distressed because they were many. Charles Ellicott: there was no ground for the fear, for God had commanded, “Distress not the Moabites” (Deuteronomy 2:9). The Reformed Gill: lest Israel do to Moab as to Sihon and Og. Wesley: the dread foretold of all nations (Deuteronomy 2:25) and of Moab (Exodus 15:15).

The verse records Moab’s fear. Moab is sore afraid and distressed for the multitude of Israel. The dread was real, though groundless.

So the witnesses see Moab seized with a great and distressing fear of Israel, though that fear had no real ground. The witnesses note the depth of the dread: “Moab was sore afraid… and Moab was distressed” — the king, his nobles, and the people were filled with terror at the multitude of Israel, fearing (as Gill says) that they would be served as Sihon and Og had been. They mark the striking point Ellicott draws out: there was in truth no ground for this apprehension, for God had expressly commanded Israel, “Distress not the Moabites, neither contend with them in battle” (Deuteronomy 2:9); Israel had no commission to attack Moab, and Moab might have dwelt in safety. They observe Wesley’s note that this very dread had been foretold — both generally, that the fear of Israel should fall on all nations (Deuteronomy 2:25), and particularly of Moab, that trembling should take hold of them (Exodus 15:15); the prophecy was fulfilled in this distress. They note the irony the witnesses see: Moab tormented itself with a fear that need never have been, and was driven by it to seek the ruin of a people who would not have touched them; groundless fear bred wicked counsel. They observe that the wicked are often pursued by terrors of their own imagining, while the people of God, whom they dread, mean them no harm. They note that Moab’s fear was the root of all the mischief that followed. The verse thus records Moab’s groundless dread — the consulting with Midian following in the next verse.
4:
On Numbers 22:4 — “And Moab said unto the elders of Midian, Now shall this company lick up all that are round about us… And Balak the son of Zippor was king of the Moabites at that time” — the witnesses mark Moab’s alliance with Midian.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: Balak may have been a Midianite imposed on Moab; the alliance of the two nations runs through the narrative. The Reformed Gill: Moab consulted the elders of Midian for their common safety. Wesley: the elders, elsewhere called kings and princes of Midian (Numbers 31:8), now united against the common enemy.

The verse joins Moab and Midian. Moab fears Israel will lick up all around, and consults Midian. Two peoples unite against Israel.

So the witnesses see Moab, in its fear, draw the elders of Midian into common counsel against Israel. The witnesses note the alarmed words: “now shall this company lick up all that are round about us, as the ox licketh up the grass of the field” — Moab pictures Israel as an ox devouring the grass, fearing that the multitude would consume and waste all the surrounding peoples; the figure marks the depth of their dread. They mark the alliance formed: “Moab said unto the elders of Midian” — Gill observes that Moab sent for and consulted the elders of Midian for the safety of both peoples; Wesley notes that these elders are elsewhere called the kings and princes of Midian (Numbers 31:8), who, though a divided people, now united with Moab against their common enemy. They observe Ellicott’s note that the close alliance of Moab and Midian runs throughout the story, and that Balak himself may have been of Midianite origin, set over Moab as king. They note the parenthesis: “Balak… was king of the Moabites at that time” — the writer marks who then reigned, the chief mover of the plot against Israel. They observe the way of the world the witnesses see: that peoples otherwise at odds will league together against the people of God, common enmity uniting them. They note that the confederacy was formed in fear, to compass Israel’s ruin. The verse thus joins Moab and Midian — the sending to Balaam following in the next verse.
5:
On Numbers 22:5 — “He sent messengers therefore unto Balaam the son of Beor to Pethor… saying, Behold, there is a people come out from Egypt… they abide over against me” — the witnesses mark the sending for Balaam.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Balaam, once a prophet of the true God, had added sorcery to his gift; the description of his dwelling fits any place. Charles Ellicott: the name Balaam from bala, “to devour.” The Reformed Calvin: Balak, conscious of his weakness, modestly seeks help — a good principle, ill turned. Wesley: Balaam is called a prophet (2 Peter 2:16), whom God inspired to speak.

The verse sends for Balaam. Balak summons the diviner Balaam from Pethor. The enemy seeks a prophet to curse Israel.

So the witnesses see Balak send to the far country for Balaam, a man of strange and mingled character, to curse Israel. The witnesses note the messenger’s errand: “there is a people come out from Egypt… they abide over against me” — Balak reports the coming of Israel and his own peril, and seeks Balaam’s help against them. They mark the much-debated character of Balaam that the witnesses weigh: Clarke and Wesley note that he was, in some sort, a prophet who knew the true God — the New Testament calls him a prophet (2 Peter 2:16) — yet one who had joined to whatever gift he had the practice of sorcery and divination, and who loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Peter 2:15); a man between two worlds, knowing God yet serving gain. They observe Ellicott’s note on his ominous name, from a root meaning “to devour” or “to consume the people.” They note Calvin’s discernment that Balak’s seeking of help, conscious of his own weakness, sprang from a principle good in itself, but turned to evil ends, as Satan ever perverts good instincts; the king sought a higher power, but the wrong one and to a wicked purpose. They observe the dwelling, Pethor by the river (Euphrates), far off, whence the famed diviner was fetched. They note that the enemy, despairing of arms, sought the weapon of a curse. The verse thus sends for Balaam — the request itself stated in the next verse.
6:
On Numbers 22:6 — “Come now therefore… curse me this people; for they are too mighty for me… for I wot that he whom thou blessest is blessed, and he whom thou cursest is cursed” — the witnesses mark Balak’s request and his faith in the curse.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Balaam was one of the Moshelim, who added sorcery to the poetic gift. Charles Ellicott: Balak undoubtedly believed in the efficacy of Balaam’s incantations; the power to bless and curse was celebrated as a great gift. The Reformed Gill: come to my country and curse them. Wesley: curse them by thy power with thy gods; “we may smite them” — thou by imprecations, I by the sword.

The verse gives the request. Balak asks Balaam to curse Israel, trusting his blessing and curse have power. The king seeks a curse to overcome a people too mighty for him.

So the witnesses see Balak make his request plainly: that Balaam curse Israel, in the firm belief that his curse would prevail where arms could not. The witnesses note the plea: “curse me this people; for they are too mighty for me” — Balak owns he cannot overcome Israel by force, and so seeks to weaken them by a curse, that he might then smite and drive them out; Wesley draws the partnership he intends — “thou by thy imprecations, and I by my sword.” They mark the king’s faith in Balaam’s power: “he whom thou blessest is blessed, and he whom thou cursest is cursed” — Ellicott observes that Balak undoubtedly believed in the real efficacy of Balaam’s incantations, and that the power to bless and curse with effect was celebrated in that age as a great and dreaded gift. They observe the heathen darkness in this: that Balak thought the favour of God could be bought or the curse of God procured by a diviner’s art, as if blessing and cursing lay in the prophet’s mouth rather than in God’s hand. They note the folly the witnesses expose: that no curse can fall on those whom God has blessed, and that the whole design was vain from the first, as the event would show (Numbers 23:8). They observe that Balak honoured Balaam’s word above its worth, ascribing to the man what belonged to God alone. They note that the request set the stage for God’s turning of the curse into a blessing. The verse thus gives Balak’s request — the messengers setting out with it in the next verse.
7:
On Numbers 22:7 — “And the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian departed with the rewards of divination in their hand; and they came unto Balaam” — the witnesses mark the messengers bearing the diviner’s fee.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: whoever consulted a prophet took a present, on which such men lived; here more than a present, perhaps all needful to procure the divination. Charles Ellicott: literally, “the divinations”; the close alliance of the two nations appears throughout. The Reformed Gill: the elders were princes and nobles, sent on this errand.

The verse sends the messengers. The elders go with the rewards of divination to Balaam. The fee is brought to buy the curse.

So the witnesses see the elders of Moab and Midian set out to Balaam, carrying the price of his divination. The witnesses note the dignity of the envoys: “the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian” — Gill observes these were princes and nobles, men of rank sent on so weighty an errand; the importance Balak set on the business appears in the quality of those he sent. They mark the fee they bore: “the rewards of divination in their hand” — Clarke notes that whoever went to consult a prophet or diviner took a present, on which such men lived; but here, Ellicott observes (rendering it “the divinations”), more than a mere gift is meant — perhaps all that was thought needful to procure and pay for the divination, the hire of the curse. They observe the alliance again displayed, the elders of both nations going together, as the close league of Moab and Midian runs through the whole story. They note the moral the witnesses see in the “rewards of divination”: that Balaam was a man to be hired, his prophecy a thing for sale, and that the wages of unrighteousness, which he loved (2 Peter 2:15), were the bait that drew him; the fee in their hand was the snare to his soul. They observe that the covetous prophet and the fearful king met over a price. They note that the love of money was the root of Balaam’s undoing. The verse thus sends the messengers with the fee — Balaam’s first answer following in the next verse.
8:
On Numbers 22:8 — “And he said unto them, Lodge here this night, and I will bring you word again, as the LORD shall speak unto me: and the princes of Moab abode with Balaam” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s consulting the LORD, with his true character disclosed.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: it appears he knew the true God and had consulted him before. Charles Ellicott: these words indicate his true character — as a prophet of the LORD he must have known that to curse Israel was sin, and should have refused outright. The Reformed Gill: he was at once inclined to do their business if he could be permitted. Calvin: waiting for a revelation from the true God, he was likely no mere sorcerer. Wesley: the night, the time of God’s dreams; “the LORD,” Jehovah, the true God.

The verse shows Balaam consulting God. He bids them lodge while he asks the LORD. His readiness betrays his heart.

So the witnesses see Balaam receive the envoys and undertake to consult the LORD, yet in a manner that betrays the doubleness of his heart. The witnesses note that he knew and consulted the true God: “as the LORD shall speak unto me” — Clarke and Wesley observe that the name is Jehovah, the true God, whom Balaam knew and had been wont to consult, receiving oracles from him; Calvin infers that, waiting for a revelation from the true God, he was likely no mere magician or sorcerer, but a man to whom God really spoke. They mark the time he chose: “lodge here this night” — Wesley notes the night was the season when God revealed his mind by dreams. They observe, however, the fault the witnesses discern even here, drawn out sharply by Ellicott: that Balaam’s very willingness to inquire betrayed his true character; for as a prophet of the LORD he must have known that to curse the people God had chosen was a plain sin, and he ought to have refused the errand outright, without asking; that he kept the envoys and sought leave shows his heart was already inclined to go, if only he might. They note Gill’s plain judgment that Balaam was at once disposed to do their business for them, and would gladly have engaged in it could he have been permitted; the consulting was not the reluctance of conscience but the angling of covetousness for a permission. They observe the danger the witnesses see: that to parley with a known sin, and ask leave for it, is already to have half-yielded. They note that the princes abode, awaiting his word. The verse thus shows Balaam consulting God — God’s question to him following in the next verse.
9:
On Numbers 22:9 — “And God came unto Balaam, and said, What men are these with thee?” — the witnesses mark God’s searching question.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: like the question to Elijah, “What doest thou here?” (1 Kings 19:9), or to Hezekiah, “What said these men?” The Reformed Gill: God came in a dream, as to Abimelech and Laban. Wesley: he asks that Balaam, repeating the matter in God’s presence, might be convinced and ashamed of his sin.

The verse gives God’s question. God comes and asks who the men are. The searching question probes Balaam’s heart.

So the witnesses see God come to Balaam and put to him a searching question about his visitors. The witnesses note the manner of God’s coming: Gill observes that God came to Balaam in a dream of the night, as he had come to Abimelech and to Laban (Genesis 20:3, Genesis 31:24); even to such a man God condescended to speak. They mark the question: “What men are these with thee?” — Ellicott compares it to the searching questions God put to Elijah, “What doest thou here?” (1 Kings 19:9), and to Hezekiah, “What said these men, and whence came they?” (Isaiah 39:3); not that God needed information, but that the question might probe the conscience of the one addressed. They observe Wesley’s discernment of the purpose: God asked, not for his own knowledge, but that Balaam, by repeating the matter aloud in God’s presence, might be brought to see and be ashamed of the sin and folly of offering his service in such a business; the question was meant to convict him, and to lay a foundation for the answer God would give. They note the mercy the witnesses see in this dealing: that God gives the covetous prophet space to consider, putting the case to his own conscience before pronouncing on it. They observe that the all-knowing God asks, not to learn, but to teach and to try. They note that the question called Balaam to weigh what he was about. The verse thus gives God’s searching question — Balaam’s answer following in the next verses.
10:
On Numbers 22:10 — “And Balaam said unto God, Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, hath sent unto me” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s answer to God.

The Reformed Gill: Balaam answered the question God put to him, naming Balak and his errand.

The verse gives Balaam’s reply. He tells God that Balak the king of Moab has sent to him. He repeats the matter before God.

So the witnesses see Balaam answer God’s question, rehearsing before him the message of Balak. The witnesses note the reply: “Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, hath sent unto me” — Gill observes that Balaam answered the question God had put, naming the king who had sent and the errand on which his messengers came. They mark that Balaam, knowing he could not deceive the omniscient God, told the matter plainly, repeating Balak’s words; he spoke truthfully of the business, whatever the corruption of his desire. They observe the very thing Wesley noted at the question (v. 9): that in being made to rehearse the errand aloud in God’s presence, Balaam was given to hear from his own mouth the wickedness of what he was asked to do — to curse a whole people for a king’s fear and a diviner’s fee; the bare telling of it ought to have shamed him. They note the irony the witnesses see: that the man stated his case fairly to God, yet without any sign of the reluctance that conscience should have felt; he laid the temptation before God almost as one seeking leave, not as one fleeing a sin. They observe that God deals with Balaam by drawing the matter out of his own lips. They note that the answer set up the plain prohibition to come. The verse thus gives Balaam’s reply — the substance of Balak’s request rehearsed in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in this brief answer, a man laying his temptation open before God yet without the recoil of conscience that should have attended it. They observe that Balaam, knowing the omniscient God could not be deceived, told the matter truthfully, naming the king and the errand; he hid nothing of the facts. They mark, however, that he spoke of the business almost as one seeking leave, not as one fleeing a plain sin; he rehearsed before God the very thing God had given him a conscience to refuse, and showed no sign of the reluctance that a holy heart would have felt at being asked to curse a people he could not but know were favoured of heaven. They note the mercy the witnesses see even in God’s questioning: that by drawing the matter out of Balaam’s own lips, God gave him to hear his errand named, and so a fresh occasion to see and shun its wickedness. They observe that the answer set up the flat prohibition that follows, leaving Balaam without excuse. They note that to state a temptation fairly is not the same as to resist it, and that the heart’s leaning is betrayed less by the words than by the absence of any horror at the sin.
11:
On Numbers 22:11 — “Behold, there is a people come out of Egypt… come now, curse me them; peradventure I shall be able to overcome them, and drive them out” — the witnesses mark Balaam rehearsing Balak’s request.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: better, “the people which came out from Egypt, it covereth…” The Reformed Gill: he faithfully relates Balak’s words, knowing he could not deceive the omniscient God.

The verse repeats the request. Balaam tells God of Balak’s wish to have Israel cursed and driven out. The temptation is laid bare before God.

So the witnesses see Balaam set out before God the full substance of Balak’s request, that Israel be cursed and overcome. The witnesses note the faithfulness of the report: “come now, curse me them; peradventure I shall be able to overcome them, and drive them out” — Gill observes that Balaam relates Balak’s words faithfully and exactly, as well knowing he could not deceive the omniscient God; he hides nothing of the errand. They mark the description of Israel: “a people come out of Egypt… it covereth the face of the earth” (so Ellicott would render); the multitude that filled Balak with dread is named again, the very greatness of Israel being the ground of the request. They observe the purpose plainly stated: that the curse was sought as a means to overcome and drive out a people Balak could not face in arms; the diviner’s word was to do what the king’s sword could not. They note that in rehearsing the matter, Balaam laid the whole temptation open before God — the people, the request, the hoped-for victory — and so had no excuse of ignorance when God should forbid him; the case was fully before the Judge. They observe the folly the witnesses see, that a king should think to overthrow by a hired curse the people whom God had brought out of Egypt with a mighty hand. They note that the request rehearsed was the very thing God would flatly deny. The verse thus repeats the request before God — the plain prohibition following in the next verse.
12:
On Numbers 22:12 — “And God said unto Balaam, Thou shalt not go with them; thou shalt not curse the people: for they are blessed” — the witnesses mark God’s flat prohibition.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “thou shalt not go with them to curse the people” — he might go (as he afterwards did by God’s command), but not to curse. The Reformed Gill: a denial of Balak’s first request. Calvin: God peremptorily removes all doubt, for it was unlawful to curse those he had blessed; the prophet can but witness God’s grace.

The verse forbids Balaam. God says he shall not go nor curse, for Israel is blessed. The curse is barred at its root.

So the witnesses see God give a plain and peremptory prohibition, forbidding both the going and the cursing. The witnesses note the double command: “thou shalt not go with them; thou shalt not curse the people” — God forbids Balaam to go with the messengers on this errand and to curse Israel; Clarke nicely observes the precise sense, “thou shalt not go with them to curse,” for Balaam might lawfully go (as he afterward did by God’s own bidding), but never to curse the people. They mark the all-sufficient ground: “for they are blessed” — Calvin draws out that God peremptorily removes all room for doubt, because it was simply unlawful, and indeed impossible, to curse those whom God had blessed; the blessing of God lay on Israel, and no diviner’s word could reverse it. They observe the truth the witnesses press, which is the heart of the whole story: that the people of God stand under his blessing, and that blessing is irreversible; what God has blessed, no man and no power of darkness can curse (Romans 8:31). They note Calvin’s further point that a true prophet can do no more than be the witness and herald of God’s grace; he cannot bestow or withdraw it at his own or another’s will. They observe that God’s first word to Balaam was clear and final, leaving no honest room for further inquiry. They note that the security of God’s people rests in his unchangeable blessing. The verse thus forbids Balaam — his report to the princes following in the next verse.
13:
On Numbers 22:13 — “And Balaam rose up in the morning, and said unto the princes of Balak, Get you into your land: for the LORD refuseth to give me leave to go with you” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s partial report.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Balaam said, “Get you into your land, for the LORD refuseth to give me leave.” The Reformed Gill: he rose with the dream impressed on his mind. The witnesses note that he told the princes less than God had told him.

The verse gives his report. Balaam dismisses the princes, saying the LORD will not let him go. He tells them less than God said.

So the witnesses see Balaam dismiss the princes, yet in his report betray the leaning of his heart. The witnesses note the dismissal: “get you into your land: for the LORD refuseth to give me leave to go with you” — Balaam sends the messengers home, owning that the LORD would not permit him to go. They mark the telling defect in his words, which the witnesses (citing Ainsworth) observe: Balaam told the princes less than God had told him; God had said, “thou shalt not curse the people, for they are blessed” (v. 12), but Balaam reports only that the LORD “refuseth to give me leave to go,” saying nothing of the blessing of Israel nor of the unlawfulness of cursing them. They observe the meaning the witnesses draw from this suppression: that Balaam, by lessening the word of God, left Balak room to hope that the prohibition was only of the going, not absolute, and that stronger inducements might yet prevail; he half-opened the door he should have shut. They note the working of covetousness in this: a man truly resolved against the sin would have told the whole truth, that Israel was blessed and the errand vain; but Balaam, loving the reward, framed his answer so as not to cut off all hope of a second offer. They observe that to soften or abridge God’s plain word, in a matter where the heart is tempted, is itself a yielding. They note that his report invited the very second embassy that followed. The verse thus gives his partial report — the princes returning to Balak in the next verse.
14:
On Numbers 22:14 — “And the princes of Moab rose up, and they went unto Balak, and said, Balaam refuseth to come with us” — the witnesses mark the report carried back to Balak.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: observe Satan’s practice against God’s word, lessening it from hand to hand — Balaam told the princes less than God told him, and they tell Balak less than Balaam. Charles Ellicott: Balaam had not told them the ground, “for they are blessed,” so Balak hoped stronger inducements might prevail. The Reformed Gill: they went without parley, perceiving he was determined.

The verse carries the report back. The princes tell Balak that Balaam refuses to come. The word is lessened again in the telling.

So the witnesses see the princes carry to Balak a report yet further softened from the truth. The witnesses note what they said: “Balaam refuseth to come with us” — they report only Balaam’s refusal, as of a man who would not come, not the LORD’s prohibition nor the blessing of Israel. They mark the striking observation Clarke makes (after Ainsworth): here is Satan’s practice against the word of God, to lessen it from hand to hand till he bring it to naught — God told Balaam the full truth, Balaam told the princes less, and the princes tell Balak less still; at each remove the word is weakened, the blessing of Israel and the unlawfulness of the curse dropping out of the report. They observe Ellicott’s point on the effect: because Balaam had not told them the real ground, “for they are blessed,” Balak was left to suppose that the refusal was a matter of the prophet’s reluctance or his price, and so might be overcome by stronger inducements; the door was left open for a second and richer embassy. They note Gill’s observation that the princes did not stay to argue with Balaam, perceiving he was determined, but went straight back. They observe the lesson the witnesses see: how the plain word of God is diminished and corrupted as it passes through unfaithful mouths, each telling of it less than the truth, to the encouraging of sin. They note that the softened report bred Balak’s fresh attempt. The verse thus carries the report back — the second embassy following in the next verses.
15:
On Numbers 22:15 — “And Balak sent yet again princes, more, and more honourable than they” — the witnesses mark the second, more honourable embassy.

The grammatical-historical The Puritan Henry: it were well if we were as earnest and constant in a good work as Balak in this ill one. The Reformed Gill: more in number and greater in quality, supposing Balaam thought himself not honoured enough. Calvin: the ungodly, however humbly they implore, do not lay aside their pride.

The verse sends a second embassy. Balak sends more and greater princes. The king presses his suit with higher men.

So the witnesses see Balak, undeterred by the first refusal, send a second and more honourable embassy to Balaam. The witnesses note the increase: “princes, more, and more honourable than they” — Gill observes that the new envoys were more in number and greater in rank, princes of the first quality in Balak’s court, sent on the supposition that Balaam had felt himself not honoured enough by the first embassy and might be won by a more dignified suit. They mark Henry’s pointed reflection: it were well for us if we were as earnest and constant in prosecuting a good work, notwithstanding discouragements, as Balak was in pursuing this evil one; the diligence of the wicked in sin rebukes the slackness of the godly in duty. They observe Calvin’s note on the spirit of the second sending: that the ungodly, however humbly they may seem to implore help, do not lay aside their pride, but trust that their grandeur and their honours can prevail; Balak thought to bend even the prophet, and (as it were) God himself, by the weight of his princes and the greatness of his offers. They note the persistence the witnesses mark in Balak: his fear and his enmity to Israel would not let the matter rest at one refusal, but drove him to press again with stronger means. They observe that the second embassy was a fresh and sharper trial to the covetous Balaam, the bait now richer and the honour greater. They note that the world’s persistence in evil is great. The verse thus sends the second embassy — its flattering message following in the next verses.
16:
On Numbers 22:16 — “And they came to Balaam, and said to him, Thus saith Balak… Let nothing, I pray thee, hinder thee from coming unto me” — the witnesses mark the second message’s entreaty.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: they said, “Let nothing hinder thee from coming.” The Reformed Gill: men of rank, they did not decline the embassy, nor think it below them to wait on the soothsayer.

The verse brings the second message. The princes entreat Balaam to let nothing hinder his coming. The suit is pressed with urgency.

So the witnesses see the second embassy come to Balaam with an urgent entreaty from Balak. The witnesses note the message: “Thus saith Balak… Let nothing, I pray thee, hinder thee from coming unto me” — the king presses Balaam to let no obstacle stand in the way, to overcome whatever scruple or hindrance had held him back; the words are full of urgency and entreaty. They mark Gill’s observation on the dignity of the messengers: though men of rank and high office, the princes did not decline so mean an errand, nor think it below them to wait upon a soothsayer; the importance Balak set on the business, and the honour in which divination was held, made even great men its suitors. They observe the flattery and pressure in the suit: Balak treats Balaam as a man of the highest consequence, whose coming is worth any cost, and whose reluctance must be coaxed away; the king stoops to entreat the diviner. They note the danger to Balaam in this honour: that a man already inclined to go was now plied with the flattering importunity of princes, his vanity courted and his covetousness inflamed; the second suit was a heavier trial than the first. They observe the way of the world the witnesses see: how the great will humble themselves to those who can serve their ends, and how dangerous such flattery is to the one courted. They note that the entreaty prepared the way for the great offer to follow. The verse thus brings the second message’s entreaty — the promise of honour following in the next verse.
17:
On Numbers 22:17 — “For I will promote thee unto very great honour, and I will do whatsoever thou sayest unto me: come therefore, I pray thee, curse me this people” — the witnesses mark the bait of honour and reward.

The Reformed Gill: Balak would promote Balaam to great honour in his court, perhaps make him prime minister; as before he had laid a bait for his covetousness with rewards, so now with honour.

The verse offers the bait. Balak promises great honour and whatever Balaam asks, if he will but curse Israel. The temptation is made strong.

So the witnesses see Balak set before Balaam the strongest bait — great honour and unlimited reward — to draw him to the cursing. The witnesses note the promise of honour: “I will promote thee unto very great honour” — Gill observes that Balak offered to advance Balaam to high dignity in his court, perhaps to make him a great officer, even his prime minister; the king dangled rank and station before the prophet. They mark the promise of reward: “I will do whatsoever thou sayest unto me” — Balak gives Balaam a blank warrant, undertaking to grant whatever he should ask, holding back nothing; the bait is made as large as the diviner’s greed could wish. They observe the two-edged temptation the witnesses see: as Balak had first laid a snare for Balaam’s covetousness with the rewards of divination (v. 7), so now he doubles the bait with honour as well as wealth, pressing upon both the love of money and the love of preferment; the trial is sharpened to the utmost. They note the one plain condition: “come therefore… curse me this people” — all the honour and reward hung on the doing of the one forbidden thing, the cursing of God’s blessed people. They observe the danger the witnesses mark for Balaam: that such an offer, to a man who loved the wages of unrighteousness, was a fierce assault on his weak point; the price was high, and his heart was for sale. They note that the great offer set the stage for his fine words and his real fall. The verse thus offers the bait — Balaam’s answer following in the next verse.
18:
On Numbers 22:18 — “If Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the word of the LORD my God, to do less or more” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s fine profession.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: Balaam knew God too well to think he could reverse his purposes, and respected him too much to act without leave; though covetous, yet he feared God. Charles Ellicott: these words may have been an ostentatious semblance of disinterestedness, or he may have been conscious of his powerlessness to curse.

The verse gives his profession. Balaam declares he cannot go beyond God’s word for a house full of silver and gold. His words are fair, his heart doubtful.

So the witnesses see Balaam answer the great offer with a noble-sounding profession of submission to God’s word. The witnesses note the fine words: “if Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the word of the LORD my God, to do less or more” — Balaam protests that no wealth, however great, could move him to act against the word of the LORD his God; the saying is true in itself and admirable on its face. They mark the truth in it that Clarke allows: Balaam knew the true God too well to suppose he could reverse his purposes, and respected him too much to dare anything without his permission; though covetous, he yet had a real fear of God, and knew that God’s word, not his own will, must rule the issue. They observe, however, the suspicion the witnesses cast on his sincerity, drawn out by Ellicott: the words may have been no more than an ostentatious show of disinterestedness and superiority to worldly gain — the very protest of one who, in his heart, longed for the silver and gold; or they may have sprung from his conscious inability to curse whom God had blessed, dressed up as virtue. They note the tell-tale phrase “the word of the LORD my God”: he names God as his, yet his next act (asking leave again) shows he hoped that very word might change. They observe the danger the witnesses see: that fair professions of submission to God may cloak a heart still set on the forbidden gain; the lips confess what the will resists. They note that Balaam’s words outran his obedience. The verse thus gives his fine profession — his second inquiry following in the next verse.
19:
On Numbers 22:19 — “Now therefore, I pray you, tarry ye also here this night, that I may know what the LORD will say unto me more” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s inquiring again.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: he did not know but God might make a further discovery, and so might innocently seek more light. Charles Ellicott: yet he knew God is not a man that he should lie or repent (Numbers 23:19); he indulged a vain hope of leave to curse. The Reformed Gill: this shows his strong inclination to go; he might and ought to have given a flat denial.

The verse shows him inquiring again. Balaam bids them tarry while he seeks more from the LORD. His second asking betrays his desire.

So the witnesses see Balaam, despite his fine words, ask leave of God a second time — a request that lays bare his heart. The witnesses note the inquiry: “tarry ye also here this night, that I may know what the LORD will say unto me more” — Balaam keeps this embassy as he had the first, and proposes to consult God again, as if there were more to be learned. They mark the charitable construction Clarke allows, that Balaam did not know but God might make some further discovery of his will, and so might innocently seek more light. But they observe the sharper and truer judgment the other witnesses press: Gill says plainly that this shows his strong inclination to go, when he might and ought to have given the messengers a flat denial, God’s mind being already fully known; and Ellicott marks the vanity of it — Balaam knew that God is not a man, that he should lie, nor the son of man, that he should repent (Numbers 23:19), and yet he indulged the empty hope that God might be brought to change his word and let him curse. They observe the working of covetousness in this second asking: a heart truly submitted would have rested in the first clear prohibition; but Balaam, still hankering after the reward and honour, went back to God as if to wring from him a different answer. They note the danger the witnesses see: that to ask God again for leave to do what he has plainly forbidden is not piety but the importunity of a divided heart. They observe that such asking tempts God and deceives the self. The verse thus shows him inquiring again — God’s permissive answer following in the next verse.
20:
On Numbers 22:20 — “And God came unto Balaam at night, and said unto him, If the men come to call thee, rise up, and go with them; but yet the word which I shall say unto thee, that shalt thou do” — the witnesses mark God’s permission given in judgment.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: his going was marked with displeasure, because he wished, for honours and rewards, to fulfil the king’s desire. Charles Ellicott: rather, “since the men have come to call thee”; only the word God speaks is he to do. The Reformed Calvin: God, far from deceitful, lets hypocrites be deluded by their own quibbles; Balaam wished God to belie himself.

The verse gives the permission. God lets Balaam go, if the men call him, but only to speak God’s word. The leave is granted in judgment.

So the witnesses see God grant Balaam the leave he craved, but as a judgment, not an approval, and bound to God’s overruling word. The witnesses note the permission: “if the men come to call thee, rise up, and go with them” — God allows the journey, on the condition that the men summon him; yet, as the next verses show, this leave was no token of God’s pleasure. They mark the binding limit attached: “but yet the word which I shall say unto thee, that shalt thou do” — Balaam might go, but only to speak what God should put in his mouth; the very permission chained him to God’s purpose, so that his going could not curse but must bless. They observe the deep truth the witnesses press about this kind of permission. Calvin says that God, though far from deceitful, justly lets hypocrites be deluded by their own quibbles; Balaam in effect wished God to belie himself, and so God gave him a leave that would only ensnare him. Clarke notes that the going was marked with the divine displeasure, for Balaam wished, for the sake of honours and rewards, to fulfil the king’s desire; the permission was a giving-up of the man to the way his own heart had chosen. They observe the solemn principle the witnesses see: that when a man will not heed God’s plain prohibition but presses for leave to sin, God may grant him his desire to his own hurt, giving him up to his heart’s lust (Psalm 81:12). They note that the permission was righteous judgment cloaked as consent. The verse thus gives the permission in judgment — Balaam’s eager setting-out following in the next verse.
21:
On Numbers 22:21 — “And Balaam rose up in the morning, and saddled his ass, and went with the princes of Moab” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s eager setting-out.

The Reformed Gill: he rose up early, not waiting for the call of the princes, which showed how eager he was to be gone, and how intent upon the journey.

The verse records his going. Balaam rises early, saddles his ass, and goes with the princes. His eagerness betrays his heart.

So the witnesses see Balaam set out with the princes, and in the very manner of his going betray the eagerness of his covetous heart. The witnesses note the haste: “Balaam rose up in the morning, and saddled his ass” — Gill observes that he rose up early, and (as the next verse will show) did not even wait for the princes to call him, as God’s permission had required (v. 20); his rising betimes and saddling his own beast showed how eager he was to be gone and how intent upon the journey. They mark the significance the witnesses draw from this: God had said he might go “if the men come to call thee,” making the journey hang on their summons; but Balaam, instead of waiting to be called, went forward of his own accord, outrunning the condition and the leave; his haste was the haste of greed, hurrying to the reward. They observe the contrast the witnesses note: a man truly reluctant, going only because God permitted, would have waited and gone slowly; but Balaam sprang up early and saddled his own ass, like one glad of his errand. They note the lesson the witnesses see: that the manner of an action often discloses the heart behind it; Balaam’s eagerness showed that, whatever his fine words, his soul was set on the gain. They observe that he went, as Peter says, after the wages of unrighteousness (2 Peter 2:15). They note that his very promptness was his sin. The verse thus records his eager going — God’s anger kindled against it in the next verse.
22:
On Numbers 22:22 — “And God’s anger was kindled because he went: and the angel of the LORD stood in the way for an adversary against him” — the witnesses mark God’s anger and the angel-adversary.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the participle “was going” denotes the continuous act — he deliberately proceeded in defiance of the warnings. The Puritan Henry: God’s opposition to Balaam in his journey. The Reformed Calvin: God permits much he does not approve; the anger was just. Wesley: because he went of his own accord, not waiting to be called; the apostle calls it the way of unrighteousness (2 Peter 2:15).

The verse kindles God’s anger. God is angry that Balaam went, and the angel stands as his adversary in the way. Judgment meets the covetous prophet.

So the witnesses see God’s anger kindled against Balaam’s going, and his angel set in the way to withstand him. The witnesses note the wrath: “God’s anger was kindled because he went” — though God had permitted the journey (v. 20), his anger now burned, which the witnesses are careful to explain. Wesley says it was because Balaam went of his own accord, with the princes, not waiting (as the permission required) to be called by them, but himself rising and pressing forward; and because he went in the wrong spirit, hankering after the reward, in the way the apostle calls the way of unrighteousness (2 Peter 2:15). Calvin meets the seeming difficulty — how God could be angry at what he had allowed — with the principle that God apparently permits much that he does not approve; the leave was a judicial giving-up, and the anger was just, falling on the corrupt heart with which Balaam went. Ellicott notes the force of the Hebrew participle, “because he was going,” the continuous act, that Balaam deliberately and resolutely proceeded in defiance of the warnings he had received. They mark the angel’s posture: “the angel of the LORD stood in the way for an adversary against him” — the same angel who elsewhere went before Israel to guard now stands against the hireling prophet as a satan, a withstander, sword in hand. They observe the irony the witnesses see: that Balaam, hurrying to curse God’s people, met God himself barring his road; the diviner who thought to control unseen powers was confronted by the Power he could not see. They note that God’s anger against secret covetousness is real, though the outward leave was given. The verse thus kindles God’s anger and sets the angel — whom the ass, in the next verse, alone perceives.
23:
On Numbers 22:23 — “And the ass saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way… and the ass turned aside out of the way… and Balaam smote the ass, to turn her into the way” — the witnesses mark the ass seeing what the prophet could not.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: when God granted visions, only those particularly concerned saw them, while others in the same company saw nothing (Daniel 10:7; Acts 9:7). The Reformed Gill: Balaam’s eyes were held that he might be rebuked by his ass, to whom God gave to see the angel.

The verse opens the rebuke. The ass sees the angel with drawn sword and turns aside; Balaam, blind to it, smites her. The beast perceives what the seer cannot.

So the witnesses see the strange and humbling turn of the story: the ass beholds the angel that her master, the famed seer, cannot see. The witnesses note the contrast: “the ass saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way, and his sword drawn in his hand” — the dumb beast perceived the heavenly adversary with his drawn sword, while Balaam, the prophet who boasted of open eyes and visions, saw nothing; Gill observes that Balaam’s eyes were held by the providence of God, that he might be rebuked and put to shame by his own ass. They mark Clarke’s note that, when God granted such visions, those alone particularly concerned saw them, while others in the same company saw nothing — as the men with Daniel and with Saul saw not what those prophets saw (Daniel 10:7; Acts 9:7); so here God opened the eyes of the beast and held the eyes of the man. They observe the ass’s right instinct: she “turned aside out of the way… into the field,” fleeing the drawn sword; the beast obeyed the warning her master would not heed. They note Balaam’s blind cruelty: “Balaam smote the ass, to turn her into the way” — ignorant of the cause, he beat the creature for the very act that was saving his life. They observe the deep humbling the witnesses see: that God made a dumb ass wiser than the seer, the beast seeing the angel and the prophet blind; covetousness had darkened the man below his own ass. They note that God can use the meanest creature to rebuke the proud. The verse thus opens the rebuke — the angel barring the way more closely in the next verses.
24:
On Numbers 22:24 — “But the angel of the LORD stood in a path of the vineyards, a wall being on this side, and a wall on that side” — the witnesses mark the angel in the narrow vineyard path.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: better, in the hollow pass of the vineyards; “a wall,” or a fence. The Reformed Gill: the ass got into a narrow path between the vineyards before Balaam could turn her back.

The verse narrows the way. The angel stands in a walled vineyard path, hemmed on both sides. The escape grows harder.

So the witnesses see the angel take a closer stand, in a narrow walled path where there was less room to turn aside. The witnesses note the place: “a path of the vineyards, a wall being on this side, and a wall on that side” — Ellicott would render it the hollow or sunken pass of the vineyards, a track running between two walls or fences; Gill observes that the ass, before Balaam could turn her back from the field, had got into this narrow path between the vineyards. They mark the tightening of the angel’s opposition: in the open field the ass could turn aside freely, but here, hemmed by walls on either hand, there was little room to escape the drawn sword; the angel pressed his adversary into a narrower place. They observe the pattern the witnesses (with Ainsworth) trace through these verses, of God’s dealing with sinners: first he mildly shows his rod, as it were, and lets them pass; then, as they persist, he straitens their way and presses harder, that they may take warning. They note the strait into which Balaam’s course was bringing him: the further he pressed against God’s will, the narrower and more dangerous his road became; obstinacy in sin meets ever closer hindrance. They observe that the ass, again seeing the angel, would be driven to a desperate shift, as the next verse shows. They note that God can hedge up the way of the rebellious with thorns (Hosea 2:6). The verse thus narrows the way — the ass crushing Balaam’s foot in the next verse.
25:
On Numbers 22:25 — “And when the ass saw the angel of the LORD, she thrust herself unto the wall, and crushed Balaam’s foot against the wall: and he smote her again” — the witnesses mark the ass crushing Balaam’s foot.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: she thrust herself to the wall and crushed Balaam’s foot, and he smote her again. The Reformed Gill: seeing the angel again with drawn sword, frightened, in the narrow path with no way to turn.

The verse presses harder. The ass, seeing the angel, crushes Balaam’s foot against the wall; he smites her again. The warning grows painful.

So the witnesses see the ass, in her terror of the angel, crush her master’s foot against the wall, and Balaam smite her the second time. The witnesses note the beast’s plight: “when the ass saw the angel of the LORD, she thrust herself unto the wall” — Gill observes that, seeing the angel again with his drawn sword, and frightened, and hemmed in the narrow path with no way to turn aside, she pressed herself hard against the wall to avoid him. They mark the hurt to Balaam: “and crushed Balaam’s foot against the wall” — in squeezing past the angel along the wall, she jammed her master’s foot against the stone; the man now felt in his own flesh the consequence of his obstinate course. They observe Balaam’s renewed cruelty: “and he smote her again” — still blind to the cause, and now angered by the pain, he beat the poor beast a second time for fleeing a danger he could not see. They note the deepening of the warning the witnesses see: the first time the ass turned into the field and Balaam lost only a little way; now he suffers bodily hurt; God’s hindrances grow heavier as the prophet persists, the rod no longer merely shown but felt. They observe the patience and the justice together: God might have slain him at once, but bore with him through warning after warning, each sharper than the last, that he might yet turn. They note that the crushed foot was a mercy in disguise, a lesser pain to save from a greater ruin. The verse thus presses harder — the angel barring the way wholly in the next verse.
26:
On Numbers 22:26 — “And the angel of the LORD went further, and stood in a narrow place, where was no way to turn either to the right hand or to the left” — the witnesses mark the way wholly barred.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke (after Ainsworth): in this carriage of the angel God shows his proceedings against sinners — first he mildly shakes his rod, then stands in a narrower place, pressing harder. The Reformed Gill: a narrower place than before, at a further distance.

The verse bars the way. The angel stands where there is no turning, right or left. The road is wholly shut.

So the witnesses see the angel take a third and closest stand, in a place so strait that there was no turning at all. The witnesses note the spot: “a narrow place, where was no way to turn either to the right hand or to the left” — Gill observes it was a narrower place than the vineyard path, the angel pressing closer still; here the ass could not turn aside in any direction, the way being wholly blocked before her. They mark the lesson Clarke draws (after Ainsworth) from the angel’s successive stands, as a picture of God’s dealings with persistent sinners: first he mildly shakes his rod and lets them go untouched; then, as they hold on, he stands in a straiter place and presses harder; at last he bars the way altogether, that they may be brought to a stand and forced to consider. They observe how the road has closed step by step upon Balaam: the open field, the walled path, and now the strait place with no escape; the prophet’s obstinacy has brought him to a dead stop, where neither his ass nor he can go forward or aside. They note the mercy yet in this severity: God shuts up the way, not to destroy at once, but to halt the sinner in his course and turn him back; the closed road was a barrier of grace. They observe that when God will stop a man, no way is left to right or left. They note that the ass, brought to this strait, can only fall down, as the next verse shows. The verse thus bars the way wholly — the ass falling under Balaam in the next verse.
27:
On Numbers 22:27 — “And when the ass saw the angel of the LORD, she fell down under Balaam: and Balaam’s anger was kindled, and he smote the ass with a staff” — the witnesses mark the ass falling and Balaam’s rage.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: she fell down under Balaam, and Balaam’s anger was kindled, and he smote her with a staff. The Reformed Gill: the third time; the angel disappeared as he moved, and at each new place the ass saw him, though her master did not.

The verse brings her down. The ass, seeing the angel, falls down under Balaam, and he beats her in rage. The third warning meets the third blow.

So the witnesses see the ass, hemmed in and terrified, fall down beneath her master, and Balaam beat her in fury. The witnesses note the beast’s last resort: “she fell down under Balaam” — Gill observes this was the third time the ass saw the angel (who, he notes, seems to have appeared and disappeared, seen by the beast at each new stand though not by the man); now, with no way to turn, she simply sank down under her rider, unable to go on. They mark Balaam’s passion: “Balaam’s anger was kindled, and he smote the ass with a staff” — for the third time he struck the creature, now not with hand or heel but with a staff, in a heat of rage; his own anger was kindled even as God’s anger was kindled against him (v. 22), but his against a faithful beast, God’s against a faithless prophet. They observe the irony the witnesses see: that the seer, blind to the angel, raged against the only one in that path who saw clearly; his fury fell on the innocent creature that had three times saved his life. They note the deepening of his fault: thrice warned by the ass’s strange behaviour, he never once stopped to ask the cause, but only beat the harder; obstinacy made him cruel and blind. They observe that a man bent on his own way will quarrel with every hindrance rather than heed it. They note that God now gives the ass a voice to answer him. The verse thus brings her down under his blows — her mouth opened in the next verse.
28:
On Numbers 22:28 — “And the LORD opened the mouth of the ass, and she said unto Balaam, What have I done unto thee, that thou hast smitten me these three times?” — the witnesses mark the speaking of the ass.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: where is the wonder? If the ass had opened her own mouth, we might marvel; but when God opens the mouth, an ass can speak as well as a man. Charles Ellicott notes that some critics, admitting the miracle, make parts of it subjective. The Reformed Calvin defends it against the scoffers. Wesley: God conferred on her the power of speech and reasoning for that time.

The verse gives the ass a voice. God opens the ass’s mouth, and she asks why he has smitten her thrice. The dumb beast rebukes the prophet.

So the witnesses see the great wonder: God opens the mouth of the dumb ass, and she speaks to rebuke her master. The witnesses note the miracle and its true author: “the LORD opened the mouth of the ass” — Wesley says God conferred on her the power of speech and reasoning for that time; Gill marks it a supernatural work, that a dumb creature, lacking the organs of speech, should utter articulate words. They mark Clarke’s pointed answer to the wonder: where is the marvel? If the ass had opened her own mouth and reasoned, we might well be astonished; but when God opens the mouth, an ass can speak as plainly as a man — the wonder is not in the beast but in the power of God, to whom nothing is hard. They observe the dispute the witnesses record: Ellicott notes that some critics, while admitting the miraculous character of the events, would make parts of them subjective or visionary rather than literal; but Calvin defends the plain miracle against the scoffers who ridicule the passage as a fable, and the New Testament settles it — “the dumb ass speaking with man’s voice forbad the madness of the prophet” (2 Peter 2:16). They note the meekness of the ass’s words: “What have I done unto thee, that thou hast smitten me these three times?” — she pleads her cause gently, asking what fault had drawn his blows; the beast reasons more justly than the man. They observe the humbling the witnesses see: God made a brute creature reprove the seer for his blind cruelty, that the proud prophet might be brought low by his own ass. They note that God can make the dumb speak to shame the wise. The verse thus gives the ass a voice — Balaam’s furious reply following in the next verse.
29:
On Numbers 22:29 — “And Balaam said unto the ass, Because thou hast mocked me: I would there were a sword in mine hand, for now would I kill thee” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s furious answer.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: “Because thou hast mocked me: would there were a sword in mine hand, for now would I kill thee.” The Reformed Gill: rather “defiled me,” by running against the wall and lying in the dust. Wesley: Balaam was not much terrified, perhaps accustomed to converse with spirits in such shapes, or so blinded by passion.

The verse gives his rage. Balaam answers the ass that she has mocked him, and wishes a sword to kill her. His fury blinds him to the wonder.

So the witnesses see Balaam answer the speaking ass not with awe but with rage, wishing to kill her. The witnesses note the charge he flings: “because thou hast mocked me” — Gill notes the word may rather mean “defiled” or “abused” me, by running him against the wall and casting him in the dust and dirt; Balaam blames the beast for the very hurts his own obstinacy had caused. They mark the violence of his wish: “I would there were a sword in mine hand, for now would I kill thee” — the prophet, in his fury, longs for a sword to slay his own faithful ass; the irony the witnesses note is sharp, for a sword was indeed near — drawn in the angel’s hand against Balaam himself, though he could not see it. They observe the strange thing the witnesses remark: that Balaam was not much terrified or astonished at his ass speaking, but answered her as if it were a thing not wholly strange. Wesley suggests he was perhaps accustomed, in his dealings with evil spirits, to such uncanny voices and shapes; or that his passion so blinded and possessed him that he lost all wonder, his rage swallowing his reason. They note the depth of his folly: that he disputed and quarrelled with a beast, threatening to kill the creature that had saved his life, while blind to the angel and deaf to the warning of God. They observe that anger and covetousness together had so darkened him that the very miracle moved him only to wrath. They note that a heart set on its own way is past astonishment and past shame. The verse thus gives his furious answer — the ass’s reasoning reply following in the next verse.
30:
On Numbers 22:30 — “Am not I thine ass, upon which thou hast ridden ever since I was thine unto this day? was I ever wont to do so unto thee? And he said, Nay” — the witnesses mark the ass’s reasoning plea.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “ever since I was thine,” literally “all thy life long”; the Targums, “from thy youth unto this day.” The Reformed Gill: the ass made a reply, as if it understood and could reason as well as speak, which is amazing.

The verse gives the ass’s plea. She reasons that she never used to behave so, and Balaam owns it. The beast argues, and the prophet must answer.

So the witnesses see the ass press her plea with reason, and Balaam forced to grant the truth of it. The witnesses note the argument she makes: “Am not I thine ass, upon which thou hast ridden ever since I was thine unto this day? was I ever wont to do so unto thee?” — she appeals to her long and faithful service; Ellicott notes the phrase means “all thy life long,” the Targums rendering “from thy youth unto this day”; the ass reasons that, having carried him faithfully all these years and never behaved so before, her strange conduct now must have some cause he had not considered. They mark Gill’s wonder at the thing: that the ass not only spoke but reasoned, replying as if she understood his words and could argue her case — a marvel beyond the mere gift of speech. They observe the justice of her reasoning: a faithful servant’s sudden change of behaviour should make a wise master ask the reason, not reach for a sword; the beast argues more soundly than the seer. They note Balaam’s admission: “And he said, Nay” — he is forced to own that she had never been wont to serve him so; his own mouth grants her plea, convicting his rashness. They observe the humbling the witnesses see: that the prophet is brought to reason with his ass and to confess himself in the wrong to her; God made the brute the teacher and the seer the scholar. They note that even now the warning was working, leading up to the opening of his eyes. The verse thus gives the ass’s plea and Balaam’s “Nay” — his eyes opened in the next verse.
31:
On Numbers 22:31 — “Then the LORD opened the eyes of Balaam, and he saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way, and his sword drawn in his hand: and he bowed down his head, and fell flat on his face” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s eyes opened.

The grammatical-historical Wesley: he fell on his face in token of reverence and submission. The Reformed Gill: his eyes, before held, were now opened; some think he had been struck blind, but rather his sight of the angel was restrained. Calvin: our senses are sustained or extinguished by God’s secret power; Balaam’s eyes were opened by a new act of grace.

The verse opens his eyes. God lets Balaam see the angel with drawn sword, and he falls on his face. The seer at last beholds what his ass had seen.

So the witnesses see God at last open Balaam’s eyes, that he might behold the angel his ass had thrice seen. The witnesses note the divine act: “the LORD opened the eyes of Balaam” — Gill observes that his eyes, which had been held (not, he thinks, struck blind, for he saw his ass and the way, but restrained from seeing the angel), were now opened; Calvin draws the lesson that the keenness of our senses is not only given by God but sustained or extinguished by his secret power, so that Balaam saw nothing till God granted a new act of perception. They mark what he then beheld: “the angel of the LORD standing in the way, and his sword drawn in his hand” — the very sight that had terrified the ass and driven her aside now burst upon the prophet; the drawn sword that had been all along against him stood revealed. They observe his sudden submission: “he bowed down his head, and fell flat on his face” — Wesley notes this was in token of reverence and submission; the proud seer, who had raged at his ass, fell prostrate before the angel, awe and fear at last laying him in the dust. They note the humbling the witnesses see: that Balaam had been more blind than his beast, and was now forced to see and to bow; the man who boasted of open eyes (as he would later, Numbers 24:4) had been outdone by a dumb ass until God opened his eyes. They observe that God can in a moment unveil to the sinner the danger he would not believe. They note that the sight brought him low. The verse thus opens his eyes — the angel’s rebuke following in the next verse.
32:
On Numbers 22:32 — “Wherefore hast thou smitten thine ass these three times? behold, I went out to withstand thee, because thy way is perverse before me” — the witnesses mark the angel’s rebuke.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “because thy way is perverse before me”, or “the way leads to destruction in my sight.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: thy way is perverse, springing from covetousness.

The verse gives the rebuke. The angel asks why he smote the ass, and tells him his way is perverse. The prophet’s sin is named.

So the witnesses see the angel rebuke Balaam, both for his cruelty to the ass and for the perverseness of his way. The witnesses note the first reproof: “wherefore hast thou smitten thine ass these three times?” — the angel takes up the very words and grievance the ass had spoken (v. 28), charging Balaam with the wrong he had done his faithful beast; the man is called to account for his blind cruelty. They mark the deeper charge: “behold, I went out to withstand thee, because thy way is perverse before me” — the angel declares himself the adversary in the path, and names the cause: Balaam’s way was perverse, crooked, contrary to God. Ellicott notes the phrase may mean “the way leads to destruction in my sight,” a road headlong to ruin; Wesley puts his finger on the root, that the perverseness sprang from covetousness — the love of the reward had bent his way crooked. They observe the justice the witnesses see in the rebuke: it was not the ass but Balaam who deserved the blows, for it was his perverse course that had brought all the trouble; the beast had done right, the master wrong. They note the mercy mingled with the reproof: the angel explains the cause of the hindrance, that Balaam may understand and turn; God reasons with the prophet as Balaam had been made to reason with his ass. They observe that God lays bare the covetous heart as the spring of the perverse way. They note that the sin is named, that it may be repented. The verse thus gives the angel’s rebuke — the ass’s saving turn explained in the next verse.
33:
On Numbers 22:33 — “And the ass saw me, and turned from me these three times: unless she had turned from me, surely now also I had slain thee, and saved her alive” — the witnesses mark the ass’s turning that saved Balaam’s life.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the angel sought to preserve Balaam from the destruction his way led to. Adam Clarke: how often are the meanest animals and trivial occurrences instruments of preserving our lives and saving our souls. Charles Ellicott: there may be an aposiopesis — “perhaps she turned aside… for otherwise now I had killed thee.” Wesley: thy anger against her was unjust, her turning was wholly for thy benefit.

The verse shows the mercy. The ass’s turning thrice saved Balaam’s life. The beast he beat had been his preserver.

So the witnesses see the angel reveal that the ass’s turning aside had thrice saved her master’s life. The witnesses note the disclosure: “the ass saw me, and turned from me these three times” — the beast had seen the angel and turned away, not from waywardness, but to avoid the drawn sword; her seeming disobedience was her wisdom. They mark the angel’s dreadful word: “unless she had turned from me, surely now also I had slain thee, and saved her alive” — had the ass gone on as Balaam wished, the angel would have killed the man and spared the beast; the very turning for which Balaam beat her had stood between him and death. They observe Wesley’s sharp point: that Balaam’s anger against the ass was unjust and unreasonable, for her turning aside and falling down was wholly for his benefit, not her own; he had punished his preserver. They note the reflection Clarke draws out: how often the meanest animals and the most trivial occurrences are made the instruments of preserving our lives and even of saving our souls; the messenger of justice would have slain Balaam but for the despised ass. They observe Ellicott’s note on the broken construction, the angel scarcely finishing the dreadful sentence of what would have been. They note the humbling the witnesses see: that the seer owed his life to the beast he despised, and that God’s providence had used a dumb ass to shield him from the sword. They observe that men often beat the very mercies that save them. The verse thus shows the saving turn — Balaam’s confession following in the next verse.
34:
On Numbers 22:34 — “And Balaam said unto the angel of the LORD, I have sinned; for I knew not that thou stoodest in the way against me: now therefore, if it displease thee, I will get me back again” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s shallow confession.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: here is proof that, though he loved the wages of unrighteousness, he still feared God, and was willing to drop the enterprise if God were displeased. The Reformed Gill: he confessed his sin in beating the ass, not being truly sensible of his covetousness and evil intention.

The verse gives his confession. Balaam owns he has sinned and offers to turn back if God is displeased. His repentance is shallow and conditional.

So the witnesses see Balaam confess sin, yet in a manner that betrays how little he truly repented. The witnesses note the confession: “I have sinned” — the words sound well, but Gill marks their shallowness: Balaam owns his sin chiefly in the beating of the ass, and in going on against the angel he did not see, but is not truly sensible of the deeper sin — the covetousness of his heart and his wicked intention in undertaking the errand at all. They mark the excuse he joins to it: “for I knew not that thou stoodest in the way against me” — he pleads ignorance, as though his fault were only that he had not seen the angel, not that his whole course was perverse; the confession is hedged with self-excuse. They observe the conditional offer to turn: “if it displease thee, I will get me back again” — Clarke finds in this a proof that Balaam, though he loved the wages of unrighteousness, still feared God, and was willing to abandon the journey if God were displeased; yet the witnesses note the hollowness even here, for a truly changed heart would have turned back gladly, not merely offered to turn if pressed; the “if it displease thee” leaves the door open to go on. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that there is a confession of the lips that does not reach the heart, an owning of sin that still clings to the gain; Balaam says “I have sinned,” as Saul and Judas would, without true repentance. They note that his offer to return was not refused but tested. The verse thus gives his shallow confession — the angel’s renewed permission following in the next verse.
35:
On Numbers 22:35 — “And the angel of the LORD said unto Balaam, Go with the men: but only the word that I shall speak unto thee, that thou shalt speak” — the witnesses mark the renewed permission and restraint.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the command of Numbers 22:20 is here repeated; the unrighteous prophet is punished by being made to reap the fruit of his own perversity. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: not a command but a permission, leaving him to his own heart’s lust; again ironically allowed to do what could not be done without sin.

The verse renews the leave. The angel bids him go, but speak only God’s word. Balaam is given up to his way, yet bound to bless.

So the witnesses see the angel send Balaam on, but under the same strict restraint, as a judgment upon his perverse persistence. The witnesses note the renewed leave: “Go with the men” — Gill and Calvin are careful to mark that this was not a command but a permission, a leaving of Balaam to follow his own heart’s lust; God again, as it were ironically, allows him to do what could not be done without sin, giving the covetous man his way to his own hurt. They mark Ellicott’s point that the command of verse 20 is here repeated, and that the unrighteous prophet is punished by being made to reap the fruit of his own perversity — he shall go, since he would go, but to no profit. They observe the binding restraint: “but only the word that I shall speak unto thee, that thou shalt speak” — Balaam is chained to God’s word; he may journey to Balak, but his mouth is not his own; he shall utter only what God puts in it, and so shall bless where he was hired to curse. They note the divine overruling the witnesses see: that God turns the very going of Balaam to the honour of Israel, making the hireling prophet His mouthpiece to pronounce blessings he would gladly have withheld; the curse is robbed of all power before it is sought. They observe the solemn warning: that God may grant a man his sinful desire, yet so order it that he gains nothing but shame. They note that Balaam goes, free in body but bound in tongue. The verse thus renews the permission and restraint — the meeting with Balak following in the next verses.
36:
On Numbers 22:36 — “And when Balak heard that Balaam was come, he went out to meet him unto a city of Moab… in the utmost coast” — the witnesses mark Balak going out to meet Balaam.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: better, “the city of Moab,” on the Arnon, the border; Sihon had held the land to the Arnon. The Puritan Henry: Balak and Balaam, confederate enemies of Israel, meet, differing in their expectations of success. The Reformed Calvin: this represents the spirit of all devoted to superstition without true fear of God, cringing to their false prophets.

The verse brings them together. Balak goes out to the border to meet Balaam. King and diviner meet against Israel.

So the witnesses see Balak go out in person to the border to welcome the diviner he had so earnestly sought. The witnesses note the meeting-place: “a city of Moab… in the utmost coast” — Ellicott would render it “the city of Moab,” on the Arnon at the very border (for Sihon had taken Moab’s land down to that river); Wesley adds it was not far from the camp of Israel, whom Balak desired Balaam to curse. They mark Balak’s eagerness: the king himself went out to meet Balaam, a mark of honour and of impatience; he could not wait, but came to the frontier to greet his hoped-for deliverer. They observe Henry’s note that here the two confederate enemies of God’s Israel are brought together, though differing in their expectations — Balak confident of success, Balaam already humbled and doubtful. They observe Calvin’s sharp reflection on the meeting: that it represents the spirit of all who are devoted to their superstitions without a sincere fear of God — how they cringe to their false prophets, flatter them, and hang upon their words, putting their trust in the diviner rather than in the living God. They note the irony the witnesses see: that Balak ran to meet a man who, all unknown to him, came bound by God to bless and not to curse; the king hastened to embrace his own disappointment. They observe that the eager honour paid to Balaam was the homage of fear and superstition. They note that the two met to plot, but God had already overruled the issue. The verse thus brings them together — Balak’s reproach following in the next verse.
37:
