On Numbers 22:37 — “Did I not earnestly send unto thee to call thee? wherefore camest thou not unto me? am I not able indeed to promote thee to honour?” — the witnesses mark Balak’s reproach to Balaam.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: Balak said, Did I not earnestly send to call thee? am I not able to promote thee to honour? The Reformed Gill: upon their meeting, Balak chides him for not coming at the first call.

The verse gives Balak’s reproach. He chides Balaam for not coming sooner and boasts of his power to honour him. The king presses his bait again.

So the witnesses see Balak greet Balaam not only with honour but with a half-reproach, pressing again the bait of preferment. The witnesses note the chiding: “did I not earnestly send unto thee to call thee? wherefore camest thou not unto me?” — Gill observes that Balak, upon meeting, complains that Balaam had not come at the first summons but had needed a second embassy; the king reproaches the prophet’s delay, as though it were mere reluctance or holding-out for more. They mark the renewed bait: “am I not able indeed to promote thee to honour?” — Balak presses again the promise of advancement, asserting his power and willingness to enrich and exalt Balaam; the king dangles once more the very lure that had drawn the covetous prophet. They observe the misjudgment the witnesses see in Balak: he supposes Balaam’s slowness was a bargaining for greater reward, never dreaming that the true cause was God’s prohibition; he reads the prophet’s hesitation by the measure of his own greed and ambition. They note the pressure now laid on Balaam: scarcely arrived, he is met with reproach and with the renewed offer of honour, his weak point assaulted afresh; the trial does not let up. They observe the irony the witnesses mark: that Balak boasts of his power to honour a man whom God alone could prosper or abase, and who could not, for all the king’s honours, speak one word but what God gave him. They note that the world’s offered honours are pressed hardest on the covetous heart. The verse thus gives Balak’s reproach — Balaam’s sobered answer following in the next verse.
38:
On Numbers 22:38 — “Lo, I am come unto thee: have I now any power at all to say any thing? the word that God putteth in my mouth, that shall I speak” — the witnesses mark Balaam’s sobered profession.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: here was a noble resolution, and he was faithful to it; though he wished to please the king and get wealth, he would not displease God to do it. The Reformed Gill: as for future things, the word God puts in his mouth, that he shall speak.

The verse gives his answer. Balaam owns he has no power to say anything but what God puts in his mouth. The diviner is bound to God’s word.

So the witnesses see Balaam, humbled by the angel, answer Balak with a sober confession of his own powerlessness. The witnesses note the disclaimer: “have I now any power at all to say any thing? the word that God putteth in my mouth, that shall I speak” — Balaam owns plainly that he has no power of his own to bless or curse, but must speak only what God gives him; whatever Balak may hope, the prophet’s mouth is not his own. They mark the truth and the resolution in it that Clarke commends: here was a noble resolution, and (the witnesses grant) Balaam was faithful to it; though he longed to please the king and gain the wealth and honour, he would not, and could not, displease God to win them; he speaks the bare truth of his bondage to God’s word. They observe, however, that this profession, true as it is, comes from a man whose heart still hankered after the reward; the bondage to God’s word was forced upon him, not freely embraced; he confesses what he cannot help. They note the comfort the witnesses see for Israel in the words: that the hired diviner, brought to curse, must himself confess before the king that he can say nothing but what God puts in his mouth — and God would put only blessing there; the enemy’s own prophet is made to disclaim all power against the people of God. They observe that the sovereignty of God over the prophet’s tongue is here owned by the prophet himself. They note that no curse could come where God would give a blessing. The verse thus gives his sobered profession — the going up to the high places following in the next verses.
39:
On Numbers 22:39 — “And Balaam went with Balak, and they came unto Kirjathhuzoth” — the witnesses mark the journey to Kirjath-huzoth.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: he went with Balak to Kirjath-Chuzoth, where the king slaughtered oxen and sheep and sent flesh to Balaam and the princes. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: a “city of streets”; Calvin notes Balaam was honourably and sumptuously received.

The verse records the journey. Balaam goes with Balak to Kirjath-huzoth. The diviner is brought to the king’s city.

So the witnesses see Balaam go on with Balak to the city of Kirjath-huzoth, where he was sumptuously received. The witnesses note the place: “they came unto Kirjathhuzoth” — Gill and Calvin observe that the name means a “city of streets”; whether a proper name or a description, it was a town to which Balak brought the prophet on the way to his purpose. They mark the honourable reception Calvin draws out: Moses goes on to relate how honourably and sumptuously Balaam was received, the king sparing no cost to welcome and gratify the diviner on whom he had set his hopes; the feasting and the gifts (named in the next verse) were the king’s courting of the prophet. They observe the danger to Balaam in this lavish welcome: the covetous man, already humbled by the angel and bound to God’s word, was now plied with the flattery of a royal entertainment, his weak point assailed by honour and good cheer; the trial was kept hot upon him. They note the providence the witnesses see: that God let Balaam go this far, and be feasted and courted, only that the blessing of Israel might be the more conspicuous when it came — pronounced by a prophet the king had honoured and hired. They observe that the pomp of the reception could not alter the word God would give. They note that the diviner was led to the place of his hoped success, and his real failure. The verse thus records the journey to Kirjath-huzoth — the sacrifices and feast following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in this stage of the journey, the king’s lavish courting of the prophet whose word he could not in fact command. They observe that the feasting and gifts at Kirjath-huzoth were Balak’s way of binding Balaam to his purpose by honour and good cheer, plying the covetous man at his weakest point even after the angel had humbled him. They note the danger of such a welcome to one already inclined to the reward: the pomp of a royal entertainment is a subtle snare, flattering the vanity and inflaming the greed of the one so honoured. They mark the providence the witnesses discern: that God permitted Balaam to be brought thus far, feasted and exalted, only that the blessing of Israel might shine the brighter when it came from the mouth of a prophet the enemy had hired and feasted. They observe that no entertainment, however sumptuous, could purchase a single word against the people God had blessed; the king spent his honours on a cause already lost. They note that the diviner was led with all ceremony to the very place of his hoped success and his certain failure, that the LORD might turn the intended curse into a blessing.
40:
On Numbers 22:40 — “And Balak offered oxen and sheep, and sent to Balaam, and to the princes that were with him” — the witnesses mark Balak’s sacrifices and feast.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: this was to gain the favour of his gods, and perhaps to propitiate Jehovah, that the end might be accomplished. Charles Ellicott: better, “Balak slew” — the word may denote slaying in sacrifice. The Reformed Gill: Balak slew them, either for sacrifice or for a feast, sending portions to Balaam and the princes.

The verse records the sacrifices. Balak offers oxen and sheep and sends portions to Balaam. Worship and feasting attend the plot.

So the witnesses see Balak offer sacrifices and make a feast, sending portions to Balaam and the princes. The witnesses note the slaying: “Balak offered oxen and sheep” — Ellicott observes the word may mean simply “slew,” and the witnesses divide on the purpose: Gill allows it was either for sacrifice or for a feast; Clarke takes it as sacrifice, offered to gain the favour of Balak’s gods, and perhaps to propitiate Jehovah too, that the end of cursing Israel might be accomplished. They mark the sending of portions: “and sent to Balaam, and to the princes that were with him” — of the slain beasts, flesh was sent to Balaam and to the envoys, a feast of welcome and of honour; the king entertained his hired prophet and his own princes at the table. They observe the mingling of religion and policy the witnesses see: Balak joins sacrifice to feasting, courting his gods and his diviner together, that the design against Israel might prosper; superstition and craft go hand in hand. They note the vanity the witnesses expose: that Balak should think to win by sacrifices and feasts the cursing of a people God had blessed, as if the favour of heaven could be bought to a wicked end. They observe that the feast honoured Balaam, but could not change his coming word. They note that the whole pomp was spent on a cause already lost. The verse thus records the sacrifices and feast — the going up to Bamoth-Baal following in the next verse.
41:
On Numbers 22:41 — “And it came to pass on the morrow, that Balak took Balaam, and brought him up into the high places of Baal, that thence he might see the utmost part of the people” — the witnesses mark the going up to Bamoth-Baal to view Israel.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: he thought Balaam must have the people in his eye when he cursed them, lest the curse miss those out of sight. Charles Ellicott: Bamoth-Baal, a height whence the camp could be seen; Hengstenberg notes Balak’s supposition. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: the high places consecrated to Baal-Peor; “the utmost part,” all the people to the remotest.

The verse brings him to the height. Balak takes Balaam up to the high places of Baal to view Israel. The cursing is to begin in sight of the camp.

So the witnesses see Balak bring Balaam, the next morning, up to a high place of Baal whence he could look upon Israel. The witnesses note the place: “the high places of Baal” — Wesley and Ellicott identify it as Bamoth-Baal, a height consecrated to the worship of Baal (Baal-Peor, the god of Moab), the first eminence on the way from the steppes whence the Israelite camp could be seen. They mark the purpose: “that thence he might see the utmost part of the people” — Balak brought Balaam where he could view the camp, and (Clarke and Wesley explain) at least its outermost part, on the heathen supposition that the diviner must have his object in sight to curse it, lest the curse fail to reach those out of view; Hengstenberg notes Balak’s assumption that seeing was needful to cursing. They observe the darkness of this notion the witnesses expose: that a curse could be aimed by the eye, and must be cast on what was seen — a superstition that bound the supposed power of the prophet to the line of his sight. They note the fitness, all unintended, of the height for what would really happen: that from this high place, set to curse, Balaam would be made to bless, looking down on the tents of Jacob and pronouncing them goodly (Numbers 24:5). They observe that the place of intended cursing became the place of God’s blessing. They note that the whole apparatus of Baal and high places could not turn God’s word. The verse thus brings him to the height — closing the chapter, with the oracles to follow.
